refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refinement clarifies that availability is a measurable, time-based metric with explicit exclusions (planned maintenance) and inclusions (safety-driven stops). High safety criticality demands that availability measurement does not mask safety-critical failures; safety shutdowns are counted as unavailability but are necessary. This prevents gaming the metric by hiding safety issues.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of system state (operational, maintenance, fault, safety-shutdown) with timestamped transitions. Post-evaluation analysis: (sum of operational intervals) / (total scheduled time) ≥ 0.97. Independent audit of logs by Safety Manager and Maintenance team.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The system shall detect and log all fault conditions that prevent productive operation within 5 seconds.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The system shall execute safe shutdown and recovery procedures without loss of critical state data.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The system shall provide real-time availability status and predictive maintenance alerts to Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The system shall minimize unplanned downtime through redundancy, fault tolerance, and rapid diagnostics.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized health monitoring and fault detection system that continuously samples vehicle and teleoperation subsystem states, logs all transitions, and triggers automated recovery or safe shutdown based on fault severity classification.
DP-B: Deploy redundant critical components (dual motors, dual communication links, dual safety controllers) with automatic failover and health heartbeat monitoring across all subsystems.

DP-C: Establish a predictive maintenance framework using historical telemetry data, machine learning-based anomaly detection, and scheduled preventive maintenance windows coordinated with Site Manager scheduling.

DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining centralized health monitoring (DP-A), selective redundancy for critical paths (DP-B), and predictive maintenance (DP-C) with integrated state machine for availability tracking.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid availability architecture comprising: (1) centralized health monitoring and fault detection with sub-100ms detection latency and automated safe shutdown; (2) redundancy for critical single-points-of-failure (dual safety controllers, dual communication links, motor health monitoring with graceful degradation); (3) predictive maintenance framework with telemetry-driven scheduling; (4) integrated availability state machine (Operational, Degraded, Maintenance, Fault, SafeShutdown) with continuous logging and real-time status reporting to Site Manager and Maintenance dashboards.

potential_sources: Health monitoring system must couple with Vehicle subsystem (motor, hydraulics, sensors) and Teleoperation subsystem (communication, control latency) to detect faults—this is necessary and well-defined.
Predictive maintenance couples with Maintenance/Quality stakeholder workflow—acceptable via SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces.
Redundancy in communication links couples with Network Link interface—acceptable, as dual paths are explicitly managed.
State machine couples with Safety Services for safe shutdown logic—acceptable, as this is a required safety function.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D avoids over-coupling by delegating fault response to Safety Services (not reimplementing safety logic) and by using standard interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking) rather than creating new cross-system dependencies. Redundancy is localized to critical paths, not system-wide.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Telemetry interface can support sub-100ms sampling and logging of vehicle and teleoperation state without introducing latency that degrades real-time control.
ASSUMPTION: Predictive maintenance data (historical telemetry) is available and sufficient to train anomaly detection models with acceptable false-positive rate (<5%).
ASSUMPTION: Dual communication links (Network Link redundancy) are available and can be managed without significant cost or complexity increase.
RISK: If telemetry sampling introduces latency, fault detection may miss transient failures or slow-developing faults, reducing effective availability.
RISK: If predictive maintenance model is poorly trained, false alarms may trigger unnecessary maintenance windows, reducing measured availability.
RISK: Redundancy adds complexity and cost; if not carefully managed, it may introduce new failure modes (e.g., failover logic bugs).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) telemetry sampling rate and latency budget (e.g., 10 Hz, <50ms end-to-end); (2) fault detection algorithm and severity classification thresholds; (3) redundancy scope (which components are dual, which are single with monitoring); (4) predictive maintenance model validation criteria (false-positive rate, lead time accuracy); (5) safe shutdown sequence and state preservation mechanism. These details will be allocated to sub-DPs at the next decomposition level.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that owns the availability KPI. It contains or orchestrates: (1) Vehicle subsystem (motors, hydraulics, sensors, local controllers); (2) Teleoperation subsystem (remote control, communication, tele-operator interface); (3) Safety Services (fault detection, safe shutdown, safety state machine); (4) Health Monitoring and Diagnostics (telemetry aggregation, fault logging, predictive maintenance). The NGHE Solution module is responsible for end-to-end availability measurement, state machine orchestration, and interface coordination across all sub-modules.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems: motor state, hydraulic pressure, sensor readings, communication link status, control latency, fault codes. Used by health monitoring to detect faults and log availability state transitions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety alerts to Safety Services and Site Manager: fault detection results, safe shutdown triggers, availability status changes, predictive maintenance alerts. Enables coordinated response and stakeholder notification.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional tasking with Site Manager and Maintenance team: receive maintenance scheduling requests, send availability status and predictive maintenance recommendations, coordinate maintenance windows to minimize unplanned downtime.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Dual communication paths for teleoperation and telemetry: primary and backup links. Health monitoring tracks link status and triggers failover if primary link fails. Redundancy is critical to availability KPI.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of the availability KPI. It integrates all subsystems and owns the end-to-end state machine, health monitoring, and availability measurement. Minimal diffusion: only interfaces with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services via standard interfaces. This is the correct architectural boundary.

Vehicle subsystem owns motor, hydraulics, and local sensors, but does not own teleoperation, safety services, or cross-system health monitoring. Assigning availability KPI to Vehicle would require Vehicle to own teleoperation and safety logic, creating unacceptable coupling and diffusion across system boundaries.

Teleoperation subsystem owns remote control and communication, but does not own vehicle hardware, safety services, or predictive maintenance. Assigning availability KPI to Teleoperation would require it to own vehicle health monitoring and safety logic, creating unacceptable diffusion.

Safety Services owns safe shutdown and safety state machine, which are critical to availability (safety shutdowns are counted as unavailability). However, Safety Services does not own health monitoring, predictive maintenance, or vehicle/teleoperation subsystems. Assigning availability KPI to Safety Services would require it to own non-safety functions, violating separation of concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the correct owner of CR-NGHE-004 (Availability KPI) because: (1) it is the top-level system integration boundary that orchestrates all subsystems; (2) it owns the end-to-end state machine and availability measurement logic; (3) it interfaces with all subsystems via standard interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link); (4) it can coordinate health monitoring, redundancy, and predictive maintenance without diffusing logic across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services; (5) it is the natural point of contact for Site Manager and Maintenance team to query and manage availability.
containment_risks: RISK: If health monitoring and fault detection logic is not cleanly separated from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, NGHE Solution may become a monolithic integration point with high coupling. Mitigation: define clear interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and ensure Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems are responsible for their own health reporting, not for availability logic.

RISK: If predictive maintenance model is tightly coupled to specific vehicle hardware or teleoperation protocols, it may not generalize across different customer configurations. Mitigation: design predictive maintenance as a pluggable module that consumes generic telemetry, not hardware-specific signals.

RISK: If redundancy (dual communication links, dual safety controllers) is not carefully managed, failover logic may introduce new failure modes. Mitigation: redundancy should be owned by NGHE Solution at the architectural level, but implementation should be delegated to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems with clear failover contracts.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine IM assignment to explicitly define: (1) Health Monitoring and Diagnostics as a sub-module of NGHE Solution, owned by System Engineer, responsible for telemetry aggregation, fault detection, and availability state machine; (2) Predictive Maintenance as a sub-module of NGHE Solution, owned by Maintenance/Quality, responsible for anomaly detection and maintenance scheduling; (3) Redundancy management as a cross-cutting concern, with Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems responsible for implementing dual components and reporting health status via Telemetry interface; (4) Safety Services as a separate module, owned by Safety Manager, responsible for safe shutdown logic and safety state machine, but NOT for availability measurement (that is NGHE Solution's responsibility). This clarifies ownership boundaries and reduces coupling.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid availability architecture) directly addresses CR-NGHE-004 (Availability KPI ≥0.97). The DP specifies how to achieve the target through health monitoring, redundancy, and predictive maintenance. The DP is measurable (sub-100ms detection latency, dual redundancy, predictive model validation) and traceable to the FR. No decoupling or missing logic detected.

diffusion_risks: Health monitoring logic must be centralized in NGHE Solution, not distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation. Risk: if each subsystem implements its own health monitoring, availability measurement becomes inconsistent and diffused. Mitigation: define Telemetry interface as the single source of truth for subsystem health.

Predictive maintenance model must be generic and subsystem-agnostic. Risk: if model is tightly coupled to specific vehicle hardware, it cannot be reused or updated without modifying Vehicle subsystem. Mitigation: design model to consume generic telemetry signals (e.g., 'motor_temperature', 'hydraulic_pressure') rather than hardware-specific signals.

Redundancy (dual communication links, dual safety controllers) must be managed at NGHE Solution level, but implemented in Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. Risk: if redundancy logic is scattered across subsystems, failover may be inconsistent or incomplete. Mitigation: define clear failover contracts and health heartbeat mechanisms.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct architectural boundary for availability KPI ownership. It integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services via standard interfaces without requiring changes to subsystem internals. Health monitoring, redundancy management, and predictive maintenance are all contained within NGHE Solution or coordinated via interfaces. No unacceptable diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability KPI is measurable via continuous telemetry logging and post-evaluation analysis. Verification is feasible: (1) instrument NGHE Solution to log state transitions (Operational, Degraded, Maintenance, Fault, SafeShutdown) with timestamps; (2) collect logs over agreed evaluation period; (3) calculate availability as (sum of Operational intervals) / (total scheduled time); (4) compare against target ≥0.97. Independent audit by Safety Manager and Maintenance team is feasible.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CR-NGHE-004 is allocated to NGHE Solution module. Sub-FRs (fault detection, safe shutdown, status reporting, downtime minimization) are allocated to Health Monitoring, Safety Services, and Predictive Maintenance sub-modules. Allocation is clear and traceable. No ambiguity in ownership or responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify telemetry sampling rate, latency budget, and fault detection algorithm thresholds. These details are necessary for detailed design and testing.
Define Health Monitoring and Diagnostics as an explicit sub-module of NGHE Solution, with clear ownership and interface contracts.
Define Predictive Maintenance as an explicit sub-module of NGHE Solution, with model validation criteria and maintenance scheduling algorithm.

Clarify redundancy scope: which components are dual (e.g., communication links, safety controllers), which are single with monitoring (e.g., motors). This is critical for cost and complexity management.
Define safe shutdown sequence and state preservation mechanism in collaboration with Safety Services. Ensure that safety shutdowns are logged and counted as unavailability (not hidden).

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-004 (Availability KPI) is a high-level, non-atomic requirement that requires decomposition into sub-FRs and sub-DPs. The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs: fault detection, safe shutdown, status reporting, and downtime minimization. Each sub-FR requires its own DP and IM assignment. DP-D (hybrid architecture) is sound and well-justified, but it is a system-level design pattern, not a detailed specification. The next decomposition level will allocate DP-D to sub-modules (Health Monitoring, Predictive Maintenance, Redundancy Management) and define detailed DPs for each sub-FR. ZIGZAG_DOWN is appropriate because the FR is decomposable, the DP is sound, and the IM boundary is correct; the next level will refine the design and allocate responsibility to sub-modules.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR explicitly lists four sub-FRs that must be addressed at the next level. DP-D is a high-level architecture that specifies the overall approach (health monitoring, redundancy, predictive maintenance) but does not detail the algorithms, thresholds, or implementation specifics. NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary, but it will be decomposed into sub-modules (Health Monitoring, Predictive Maintenance, Safety Services integration) at the next level. Proceeding to the next level is justified because: (1) the FR is clearly decomposable; (2) the DP is sound and addresses all sub-FRs; (3) the IM boundary is correct and will be refined, not changed; (4) the gate review passes all three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM); (5) the next level will provide the detail necessary for detailed design and testing.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The system shall detect and log all fault conditions that prevent productive operation within 5 seconds.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The system shall execute safe shutdown and recovery procedures without loss of critical state data.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The system shall provide real-time availability status and predictive maintenance alerts to Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The system shall minimize unplanned downtime through redundancy, fault tolerance, and rapid diagnostics.

assumptions: Telemetry interface can support sub-100ms sampling and logging without introducing latency that degrades real-time control.
Predictive maintenance data (historical telemetry) is available and sufficient to train anomaly detection models with acceptable false-positive rate (<5%).
Dual communication links (Network Link redundancy) are available and can be managed without significant cost or complexity increase.
Safety Services module is responsible for safe shutdown logic; NGHE Solution is responsible for availability measurement and coordination.
Site Manager and Maintenance team have access to real-time availability dashboards and can act on predictive maintenance alerts within agreed SLAs.
Evaluation period for availability measurement is at least 30 days of continuous operation to ensure statistical significance.
open_risks: Telemetry sampling latency may degrade real-time control performance. Mitigation: define latency budget (e.g., <50ms) and validate in detailed design.

Predictive maintenance model may have high false-positive rate, triggering unnecessary maintenance and reducing measured availability. Mitigation: validate model with historical data and define acceptable false-positive threshold (e.g., <5%).
Redundancy (dual communication links, dual safety controllers) may introduce new failure modes (e.g., failover logic bugs). Mitigation: design failover logic carefully and test extensively.

Health monitoring logic may become a monolithic integration point with high coupling to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. Mitigation: define clear interfaces and ensure subsystems are responsible for their own health reporting.

Availability measurement may be gamed by hiding safety-critical failures or by manipulating maintenance windows. Mitigation: independent audit by Safety Manager and Maintenance team; define clear rules for what counts as unavailability.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: sampling rate, latency budget, signal definitions, logging format.
Fault detection algorithm specification: detection thresholds, severity classification, response actions.
Redundancy design: which components are dual, failover logic, health heartbeat mechanism.
Predictive maintenance model specification: input signals, anomaly detection algorithm, validation criteria, maintenance scheduling algorithm.
Safe shutdown sequence specification: state preservation mechanism, recovery procedure, logging.
Availability measurement specification: state machine definition, logging format, post-evaluation analysis procedure.
Test plan for availability KPI: how to validate that ≥0.97 availability is achievable; test scenarios for fault detection, failover, recovery, predictive maintenance.
Customer acceptance criteria: definition of 'evaluation period', acceptable false-positive rate for predictive maintenance, maintenance window constraints.
